uh i'm uh going through uh stage

uh i'm holding up pretty well it it started with stage one colon cancer and uh i had surgery
they thought they got it all but they didn't and the one turned into four
two years later so um cancer's in my family my mother my grandmother my uh uncle
so i'm taking it in stride uh because i know i can't do anything about it anyway but i don't i don't worry i just uh live it every day
uh to this fullest
can you tell me

uh you know it's important to get uh your annual checkups and that's how i found out i had uh

cancer well that was the beginning of it um i had a blood test and my psa was high and the nurse scared me because she like oh my god and i'm like what do you mean oh my god and uh that just started the whole ball rolling there
uh getting a blood test

before you had cancer before you went and got your diagnosis what was your general perception of cancer that you kinda thought about it thought about people that had it know what was your general thought of it um my thought about cancer was um
where my my mother my uncle he was an underwater welder in new orleans so he got cancer from that bit breathing uh fumes of
uh welding and my mother and grandmother was heavy smokers and drinkers so they got it through that so
uh i never thought that i would get it too
uh but but i did so

so can you tell me after kind of like getting your diagnosis you know what was like your perception of cancer as you changed i know you say you didn't quite think you would get it um what did you kinda think about it once you kind of had it yourself okay uh once i
i accepted the fact that i'm i've got stage four instead of one
uh my perception my perception was still to stay strong my uncle uh he used to be a doctor here years ago and he recommended uh this cancer center he said it's the best in kalamazoo so he recommended
that i uh do it throughout my journey here so it's been very
uh this is a good hospital it's been very uh
supportive and helpful and uh good care quality care so i'm i'm thankful to to be able to uh
walk this journey and know that i'm not alone
and uh that i got support

can you tell me kinda going off that point tell me about how your experience has been at bronson you know working with your doctors working with your nurses you know how has treatment been for you like working with the staff and things like that okay uh this hospital is uh for me top notch uh i was at uh henry ford and
uh mclaren in hinsdale and

the quality was good but it wasn't this good i mean they're more hands on uh like my uh cancer prescriptions that came in the mail last night uh before i'm out they're calling me to tell me that you're getting low so that type of uh care is uh top notch
um how
how how long have you been in treatment now at bronson here uh at bronson i only been six months six months yeah six months since i've
switched over from uh henry ford to rochester

and can you tell me tell me maybe something you know people don't expect to go through uh cancer treatment whether it might be the medication or maybe how you feel whether it might be your energy wise you know can you just tell me what's something that people don't usually expect when they're going through cancer treatment uh for me
uh i didn't expect the uh the neuropathy i got neuropathy in my hands and my feet and that was part of the the chemo
uh they discontinued that but the uh the problem is here now but uh
that that particular
treatment it was nausea and
uh no appetite uh
it was just uh terrible experience uh at first but it's things have uh mellowed out and uh i don't have any uh outward symptoms other than the neuropathy
and uh if you didn't know if i didn't tell you that i had cancer you wouldn't be able to tell like my mother and my grandmother everybody else in my family they they were sickly and bedridden and uh
i don't know if they had four stage four but
uh i don't have those type of symptoms which i'm thankful for

so i wanna move on to how you kind of been staying so positive i i've i when i first met you you were just already just a very positive person even that very like small amount of time you know um can you tell me how you've been able to kinda remain positive not being bitter despite going through your uh the the terminal diagnosis you know what kinda keeps you on the positive side of things uh well uh my age for one you know i'm i'm i'm older now so i look at uh
life differently now
uh i appreciate every
day that i'm blessed to wake up to see another day so i don't wake up moping and moaning because uh of my condition i just uh
you know no matter how

uh i have trouble opening a a candy bar because i can't feel but i still move on and i'm thankful
and blessed to have the support of family and and that gives me strength too
uh the family support

people will fly in from my family

and that's
uh riding a bike the

so that's uh four days out of the week i stay active uh and and my body feels better
uh being active you know uh they teach you about nutrition and you know i just try to do what i can to uh help myself stay healthy

and uh it was a individual there that

prolong your life so uh i was glad that i was able to talk him into uh doing chemo and uh
you know in in in the yoga
classes uh that's uh cancer survivors mostly in there so we support each other weekly

uh whenever somebody's down just to stay supportive and uh positive you know i i can't feel their pain but i can share my pain and my journey and hopefully uh somebody else can get something out of them
that's amazing

well you need to pay attention to
uh

uh by their dedication

i would tell them to never lose hope even when you don't have hope
and
you may be going through
roadblocks and obstacles
uh uh in your journey
but the the brick walls that you run into
could be there for support instead of a barrier
so i look at my brick walls

and i'm able to move forward and you have to have that mentality
to

me wanting answers
